medications.indication_rationale: UDCA for PBC is a hepatology/GP decision at routine review, not an acute telehealth prescription tonight.
Not appropriate to start via telehealth for a self-limiting panic attack.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Hand the phone to your spouse, do paced/grounding breathing, and stop researching the condition tonight.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Removes the trigger and terminates the panic cycle.

patient_education_points.point: Your racing heart, fast breathing and sense of unreality are a panic attack. Early PBC (a positive antibody with a normal liver scan) is a very slow condition that takes years to decades to cause problems — your liver is not failing tonight.

There's no yellowing, no confusion and no fever, so this is not an emergency. Do slow breathing, stop the online research, and see your GP/hepatologist in the next couple of weeks about long-term monitoring and a protective tablet.
Go to ED if your eyes/skin turn yellow, you become confused or drowsy, you develop a high fever with shaking chills, or you get severe constant right-upper-abdominal pain.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: LFTs/fibroscan, PBC monitoring and ursodeoxycholic acid discussion; health-anxiety support.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Jaundice, confusion, fever/rigors, or severe RUQ pain — ED.

must_include_items: Screen jaundice/encephalopathy and biliary sepsis before reassuring
Explain early AMA-positive PBC is slow/benign and not failing tonight
De-escalate the panic (paced breathing, stop researching)
Liver safety-net and routine hepatology follow-up
errors_of_commission: Reflex ED dispatch for a well, anicteric, clear-minded patient
Telling her the antibody means imminent liver failure
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: T2 case: passing requires a hepatic/biliary red-flag screen + accurate reassurance + a liver safety-net. Over-triage or unscreened dismissal fail.
